question: A derivative is a financial instrument with a value derived from a:

options.A: regulatory body such as the FTC.

question: Derivatives can be used to either hedge or speculate. These strategies:

options.C: spread or minimize risk in both cases.
options.D: offset risk by hedging and increase risk by speculating.
options.E: offset risks by speculating and increase risk by hedging.

question: A forward contract is described as agreeing today to either purchase or sell an asset or security:

options.A: at a later date at a price to be set in the future.
options.B: today at the current market price.
options.C: at a later date at a price set today.
options.D: if it is advantageous to do so in the future.
options.E: with delivery today and payment in the future.

question: The buyer of a forward contract will be:

options.A: taking delivery of the goods today at today's price.
options.B: making delivery of the goods at a later date at that date's price.
options.C: making delivery of the goods today at today's price.
options.D: taking delivery of the goods at a later date at a pre-specified price.
options.E: deciding on a future date whether or not to take delivery at a pre-specified price.

question: The main difference between a forward contract and a cash transaction is:

options.A: a forward contract provides an option while a cash transaction is an obligation.
options.B: a forward contract is fulfilled at a later date while the cash transaction is carried out immediately.
options.C: the price of a forward contract is decided at a later date while a cash transaction occurs at the current spot rate.
options.D: a cash transaction can be reversed but a forward contract cannot.
options.E: the forward contract can be negotiated while a cash transaction cannot.

question: Which one of the following is not associated with forward contracts?
A potential disadvantage of forward contracts versus futures contracts is:

options.A: the extra liquidity required to cover the potential outflows that can occur prior to delivery.
options.B: the higher incentive for a particular party to default.
options.C: that the buyers and sellers don't know each other and never meet.
options.D: the obligatory requirements rather than the optional opportunities.
options.E: the increased ability to close out a position prior to expiration.

question: A 3-month futures contract on gold is priced at $1,200 per troy ounce when the contract is initiated. If the price of gold rises every day over the 3-month period, then when the contract is settled, the buyer will ________ and the seller will ________.
Futures contracts contrast with forward contracts by:

options.A: providing an option for the buyer rather than an obligation.
options.B: marking to the market on a weekly basis.
options.C: allowing the seller to deliver any day during the delivery month.
options.D: allowing the parties to negotiate the contract size.
options.E: requiring contract fulfillment by the two originating parties.

question: Futures contracts:

options.B: require delivery on a specific date.
options.E: marked to market on a weekly basis.

question: Which one of these parties would generally have the most reason to take a short hedge position in the agricultural futures market?
A miller who needs wheat to mill into flour most likely uses the futures market for taking a:

options.A: long hedge position to lock in production costs.
options.B: short hedge position to lock in delivery.
options.C: long hedge position to lock in a sales price for flour.

question: The party most apt to take a long position in agriculture futures is the firm that:
If the producer of a product has entered into a fixed price sale agreement for that output, the producer generally faces:

options.A: a nice steady profit because the output price is fixed.
options.B: an uncertain profit if the input prices are volatile. This risk can be reduced by a short hedge.
options.C: an uncertain profit if the input prices are volatile. This risk can be reduced by a long hedge.
options.D: a modest profit if the input prices are stable. This risk can be reduced by a long hedge.
options.E: a modest profit if the input prices are stable. This risk can be reduced by a short hedge.

question: You hold a futures contract to take delivery of U.S. Treasury bonds in 6 months. If the entire term structure of interest rates shifts down over the 6-month period, the value of the forward contract will have ________ the date of delivery.

options.C: the same value as when obtained on
options.D: either decreased in value or have a zero value by

question: Mortgage bankers earn income principally by:

options.C: offsetting long and short hedge positions in Treasury futures.

question: To protect against interest rate risk, the mortgage banker who has committed to lending funds but has yet to raise those funds should:

options.A: buy futures, as this position will hedge losses if rates rise.
options.B: sell futures, as this position will hedge losses if rates rise.
options.C: sell futures, as this position will add to his gains if rates rise.
options.D: buy futures, as this position will add to his gains if rates rise.

question: Futures market transactions are commonly used to reduce risk. The most risk reduction can be obtained when the asset at risk and the futures contract:
Hedging in the futures markets can reduce all risk if:

options.A: price movements in both the cash and futures markets are perfectly correlated.
options.B: price movements in both the cash and futures markets have zero correlation.
options.C: price movements in both the cash and futures markets are less than perfectly correlated.
options.D: the hedge is a short hedge, but not a long hedge.
options.E: the hedge is a long hedge, but not a short hedge.

question: Comparing long-term bonds with short-term bonds, long-term bonds are ________ volatile and therefore experience ________ price change than short-term bonds for the same interest rate shift.
When interest rates shift, the price of zero coupon bonds are ________ volatile ________

options.A: more; if they have a short maturity rather than a long maturity.
options.B: not; because their duration always matches their maturity.
options.D: less; than coupon bonds of the same maturity.
options.E: more; than coupon bonds of the same maturity.

question: The duration of a pure discount bond is:

options.B: less than that of a comparable coupon bond.
options.D: is equal to the bond's maturity.

question: In percentage terms, higher coupon bonds experience a ________ price change compared with lower coupon bonds of the same maturity given a stated change in market interest rates.
A bond manager who wishes to hold the bonds with the greatest potential price volatility should acquire:
Which one of these bonds has the highest duration?
Which one of these bonds will have the lowest percentage price change for a stated shift in interest rates?
A financial institution can hedge its interest rate risk by:

options.A: matching the duration of its assets to the duration of its liabilities.
options.B: setting the duration of its assets equal to half that of the duration of its liabilities.
options.C: matching the duration of its assets, weighted by the market value of its assets with the duration of its liabilities, weighted by the market value of its liabilities.
options.D: setting the duration of its assets, weighted by the market value of its assets to one half that of the duration of the liabilities, weighted by the market value of the liabilities.
options.E: setting the duration of its assets equal to 1.0.

question: Suenette wants to own bonds but also wants their market values to remain as steady as possible. Which type of bonds are best suited to her wishes?
The duration of a coupon bond is:

options.A: equal to its number of payments.
options.B: less than that of a zero coupon bond of equal maturity.
options.C: equal to the zero coupon bond of the same maturity.
options.E: increases as the time to maturity decreases.

question: A financial institution has equity equal to one-tenth of its assets. If its asset duration is currently equal to its liability duration, then to immunize, the firm needs to:

options.A: decrease the duration of its assets.
options.B: increase the duration of its assets.
options.C: decrease the duration of its liabilities.
options.E: increase either the duration of its assets or of its liabilities.

question: If a financial institution has equated the dollar effects of interest rate risk on its assets with the dollar effects on its liabilities, it has engaged in:
Duration is a measure of the:

options.A: yield to maturity of a bond.

question: A swap is an arrangement for two counterparties to:
LIBOR stands for:
Interest rate swaps allow one party to exchange a:

options.A: floating interest rate for a fixed rate.
options.B: fixed interest rate for a lower fixed rate.
options.C: floating interest rate for a lower floating rate.
options.D: floating interest rate for a one-time immediate cash payment.
options.E: fixed interest rate for a one-time future cash payment.

question: A U.S. firm involved in foreign exports is most apt to highly engage in:
An inverse floater and a super-inverse floater probably become the most valuable to a purchaser when:

options.D: their maturities are shorter rather than longer.

question: Assume a firm has a floating-rate loan and purchases a 10 percent cap on that loan. As a result, the firm will receive payments equal to:
Caps and floors are used in conjunction with derivatives to:

options.A: limit any impact from interest rate changes.
options.B: increase the rate of return to the derivative holder.
options.C: increase the volatility of the at-risk asset.
options.D: offset the costs associated with establishing the derivative position.
options.E: lower acquisition costs irrespective of financing costs.

question: Credit default swaps:

options.C: are traded only on national exchanges.
options.D: are rarely used in actual practice.
options.E: must follow the structure outlined by the SEC.

question: Credit default swaps are most like:
There are always at least ________ counterparties in a credit default swap.
Which one of the following is true about the use of derivatives?

options.A: Derivatives usually appear explicitly in the financial statements.
options.B: Academic surveys account for much of our knowledge of corporate derivatives use.
options.C: Small firms are more likely to use derivatives than large firms.
options.D: The most frequently used derivatives are commodity and equity futures.
options.E: Derivatives are primarily used by firms that have easy access to capital markets.

question: Today, you purchased two natural gas futures contracts at the settle price for a May delivery. The contract size is 10,000 MMBtu with quotes in dollars per MMBtu. The day's high was
954, the low was 2.939, and the settle was 2.948. What was the total amount you had to pay today?
explanation: Daily mark-to-market payments will start with the next trading day with the final payment occurring at delivery. No payment is required at time of purchase.

question: Assume you write a futures contract on corn at $4.05 per bushel. Over the next 5 trading days the contract settled at $4.07, $4.05, $4.04, $4.02, and $4.04. You then decide to reverse your position in the futures market on the fifth day at close. What is the net amount you receive on this contract per bushel?
explanation: Net amount received = −$.02 +.02 + .01 + .02 − .02 + 4.04 Net amount received = $4.05

question: Assume you write a futures contract on corn at $3.74 per bushel. Over the next 5 days the contract settles at $3.68, $3.71, $3.67, $3.64, and $3.61. Before you can reverse your position in the futures market you are notified to complete delivery on Day 5. What will you receive on delivery per bushel and what is the net amount per bushel you receive in total?
explanation: Delivery is made at the settle price of $3.61. Net amount received = $.06 − .03 + .04 + .03 + .03 + 3.61 Net amount received = $3.74

question: You bought a futures contract on corn for $3.55 per bushel and closed the contract five days later at $3.56. The daily closing prices were $3.57, $3.54, $3.53, $3.58, and $3.56. What was the mark-to-market sequence of payments per bushel from (+) and to (−) the clearing house?

Today, you purchased a futures contract obligating you to purchase 100 troy ounces of gold for $1,218 per ounce any time over the next month. Assume the spot price of gold falls to $1,216 tomorrow. What will be your cash flow tomorrow for this contract?

Last week, you sold a futures contract on 5,000 troy ounces of silver at a settle price of $16.59. Today, you made delivery and the daily settle price was $16.62. What amount will you receive at the time of delivery? Assume yesterday's settle price was $16.66.
explanation: Amount received at delivery = 5,000($16.62) Amount received at delivery = $83,100

question: Assume a futures contract on gold is based on 100 troy ounces with prices quoted in dollars per troy ounce. Assume one contract called for delivery some time during the month of April. The price of gold opened the month at $1,194. The low quote for April was $1,189, the high was $1,212, and the end of month settle quote was $1,197. By what amount did the value on one contract vary over the month of April?

Assume the futures contracts on silver are quoted in dollars per troy ounce with a contract size of 5,000 troy ounces. Contract quotes for the day included an open value of $16.650, a high of $16.660, a low of $16.620, and a settle of $16.645. If you purchased three contracts at the closing price what was the dollar cost of your purchase ignoring all transaction costs?

Assume a bank has a $25 million mortgage bond risk position which it hedges in the Treasury bond futures market. Approximately how many futures contracts would be needed for this hedge if you assumed mortgage bonds and Treasury bonds were perfectly correlated?
explanation: Number of futures contracts = $25,000,000/$100,000 Number of futures contracts = 250

question: A mortgage banker has forward contracts to lend $12 million at 4.5 percent for 15 years. What position in Treasury bond futures does this banker need to hedge the interest rate risk?
explanation: Number of short positions = $12,000,000/$100,000 Number of short positions = 120

question: Assume a bond matures in 2 years, has a coupon rate of 6 percent, pays interest annually, and has a face value of $1,000. What is the duration of this bond if it is priced at par?
explanation: Since the bond is selling at par, the market rate of interest equals the coupon rate. Duration = [($60/1.06)(1) + ($1,060/1.062)(2)]/$1,000 Duration = 1.94

question: Firm A is paying $300,000 in fixed interest payments a year while Firm B is paying LIBOR plus 30 basis points on $5 million loans. The current LIBOR rate is 5.5 percent. Firm A and B have agreed to swap interest payments. What is the net payment between these firms this year?

options.A: Firm A pays $10,000 to Firm B
options.B: Firm B pays $10,000 to Firm A
options.C: Firm B pays $12,500 to Firm A
options.D: Firm A pays $15,000 to Firm B
options.E: Firm B pays $15,000 to Firm A

explanation: Firm B pays a fixed payment of $300,000 to A in exchange for the floating payment of $290,000 (= [(.055 + .0030)($5,000,000)]). The required net payment is $10,000 paid by Firm B to Firm A.

question: Calculate the duration of a $1,000 zero-coupon bond with a current price of $455.59, a maturity of 6 years, and a yield to maturity of 14 percent.
explanation: The duration of a zero-coupon bond equals to its maturity: Duration = 6 years

question: Calculate the duration of a $1,000 face value bond with annual coupon payments, a coupon rate of 7 percent, a maturity of 4 years, and a yield to maturity of 8.2 percent.
explanation: Coupon payment = .07($1,000) Coupon payment = $70 Duration = 3.6159, or 3.62 years as shown in the table below: Time Period Cash Flow PV of Cash Flow (8.2%) Relative Value Duration 1 $ 70 $

Small Town Bank has total assets with a market value of $14.23 million and a duration of
64 years. The bank's liabilities equal $12.87 million and its equity is $1.36 million on a market value basis. To hedge interest rate risk, what duration should the bank seek for its liabilities?
explanation: Duration of assets × Market value of assets = Duration of liabilities × Market value of liabilities Duration of liabilities = 2.64($14.23 million)/$12.87 million Duration of liabilities = 2.92 years

question: You have gathered the following market value and duration information on the Eastern Bank: Assets: Market value (in millions) Duration Overnight money $
1 .0 1-year Treasury Securities
4 .6 Loans
1 Mortgages
6 $ 38.4 Liabilities: Checking accounts $ 14.6 .0 Short-term CD's
1 .3 Long-term CD's
1 $ 30.3 Calculate the duration of the bank's assets and of its liabilities.
You have gathered the following market value and duration information on Northern Bank: Assets: Market value (in millions) Duration Overnight money $
7 .0 1-year Treasury securities
2 .5 Loans
8 Mortgages
9 $ 35.4 Liabilities: Checking accounts $ 13.9 .0 Short-term CD's
5 .4 Long-term CD's
5 $ 29.3 What new asset duration will immunize the balance sheet?
explanation: DL = (13.9/29.3)(0) + (4.5/29.3)(.4) + (10.9/29.3)(3.5) DL = 1.36 years Duration of assets × Market value of assets = Duration of liabilities × Market value of liabilities Duration of assets = 1.36($29.3 million)/$35.4 million Duration of assets = 1.13 years

question: The futures markets are considered by some to be highly risky and equivalent to gambling. Why is this an inaccurate portrayal of the market's function? Answer: ∙ The market sets (discovers) the price on an exchange; thus, prices are considered to be fair. ∙ Futures can be used to hedge positions in other assets thereby reducing overall risk. ∙ Positions are marked-to-market daily which reduces the probability of one party defaulting. ∙ The futures market is generally liquid so positions can be closed out at any time. ∙ The futures market is a zero-sum game; every $1 loss is offset by a $1 gain. Difficulty: 2 Medium Section: 25.3 Futures Contracts Topic: Futures and forward contracts Bloom's: Analyze AACSB: Analytical Thinking Accessibility: Keyboard Navigation

Identify several of the differences between a forward contract and a futures contract. Answer: Forward contracts: Off-exchange transaction Individually negotiated contract terms Single-day delivery Fairly illiquid market Futures contracts: Trade on an exchange Standardized contract specifications Marked-to-market daily Delivery on any day within the delivery month Buyers generally selected at random Lower probability of default Generally a liquid market Difficulty: 2 Medium Section: 25.3 Futures Contracts Topic: Futures and forward contracts Bloom's: Apply AACSB: Knowledge Application Accessibility: Keyboard Navigation 28 Copyright © 2019 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. No reproduction or distribution without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education.

Duration is defined as the weighted average time to maturity of a financial instrument. List at least four other key things you know about duration. Answer: ∙ Low coupon bonds have a higher duration than high coupon bonds with the same time to maturity. ∙ Long-term bonds have a higher duration than short-term bonds with the same coupon rate. ∙ Zero-coupon bonds have a duration equal to their time to maturity. ∙ The percentage price change of a bond with a high duration is greater than the percentage price change of a bond with a low duration given a stated change in market interest rates. ∙ Matching the duration of assets and liabilities will hedge interest rate risk. Difficulty: 2 Medium Section: 25.6 Duration Hedging Topic: Duration hedging Bloom's: Apply AACSB: Knowledge Application Accessibility: Keyboard Navigation

Explain why credit default swaps act like an insurance policy. Answer: If you obtain insurance, you pay periodic premiums to the insurance company in exchange for the insurance company paying your claims (losses). With a credit default swap, one counterparty pays periodic payments to the second counterparty in exchange for the second counterparty paying the par value of a bond if and when the bond defaults (losses).
